items.subtopic: Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS
items.title: Understanding Parkinson’s Disease Prevalence and Demographics
items.content: Parkinson's disease (PD) is the second most common age-related neurodegenerative disorder, with an estimated 10.8 million people affected worldwide. The prevalence of PD is expected to double in the next 20 years due to population aging. The mean onset age is around 60 years, with a lifetime risk of approximately 3% for men and 2% for women.
keyPoints: PD is the second most common age-related neurodegenerative disease after Alzheimer's disease (AD).
~10.8 million people worldwide are affected by PD.
The number of PD cases is expected to double in 20 years due to aging populations.
Mean onset age for PD is about 60 years.
Lifetime risk: ~3% for men, 2% for women.

items.subtopic: Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS
items.title: Clinical Features and Pathology of Parkinson’s Disease
items.content: The cardinal clinical features of PD include bradykinesia, rest tremor, rigidity, and gait dysfunction with postural instability. Pathologically, PD is characterized by degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc), reduced striatal dopamine, and Lewy bodies containing α-synuclein. Nonmotor features such as autonomic disturbances and cognitive impairment are linked to nondopaminergic pathology.
keyPoints: Cardinal clinical features: bradykinesia, rest tremor, rigidity, gait dysfunction with postural instability.
Pathology includes degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the SNc, reduced striatal dopamine.
Lewy bodies containing α-synuclein are a hallmark feature.
Nonmotor features linked to nondopaminergic pathology.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS

items.subtopic: Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS
items.title: Early Detection and Classification of Parkinson’s Disease
items.content: Epidemiologic studies suggest that nonmotor symptoms like constipation, anosmia, REM behavior sleep disorder, and cardiac denervation may precede classic motor features by years or decades. These are considered early premotor forms of PD. New classifications based on α-synuclein accumulation and neurodegeneration distribution aim to define early stages of PD.
keyPoints: Nonmotor symptoms such as constipation, anosmia, REM behavior sleep disorder may precede motor features by years or decades.
These nonmotor symptoms are considered early premotor forms of PD.
New classifications based on α-synuclein accumulation and neurodegeneration distribution define early stages.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Parkinson's Disease
items.content: The diagnosis of Parkinson's disease (PD) has evolved from historical criteria to more accurate methods. Initially, PD was diagnosed based on the presence of two out of three parkinsonian features: tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia. However, this method had a 24% error rate in postmortem studies. The U.K. Brain Bank Criteria improved diagnostic accuracy by requiring parkinsonism (bradykinesia and rigidity) with rest tremor, asymmetry of motor impairment, and a good response to levodopa, achieving >90% pathological confirmation. The MDS Clinical Diagnostic Criteria further refine diagnosis by incorporating supportive criteria, absolute exclusion criteria, and red flags, delineating two levels of certainty: clinically established PD and clinically probable PD.
keyPoints: Historical diagnostic criteria had a 24% error rate.
U.K. Brain Bank Criteria achieve >90% pathological confirmation.
MDS Clinical Diagnostic Criteria include supportive criteria, absolute exclusion criteria, and red flags.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Imaging in Parkinson's Disease Diagnosis
items.content: Brain imaging using PET or SPECT can aid in diagnosing PD by showing reduced and asymmetric uptake in the striatum, particularly in the posterior putamen with relative sparing of the caudate nucleus. These findings reflect the degeneration of nigrostriatal dopaminergic neurons. Imaging is useful for diagnostic uncertainty, such as early-stage disease or distinguishing from other tremors, but is not routinely required unless a disease-modifying therapy becomes available. Increased iron in the SNc detected by transcranial sonography or special MRI protocols may suggest PD or prodromal PD.
keyPoints: PET or SPECT shows reduced uptake in the striatum, especially posterior putamen.
Imaging is useful for diagnostic uncertainty but not routinely required.
Increased iron in SNc detected by transcranial sonography or MRI suggests PD.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Biomarkers for Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Efforts to develop biomarkers for PD focus on detecting abnormal alpha-synuclein deposits. The alpha-synuclein seeding amplification assay (SAA) is a novel method providing high sensitivity and specificity, distinguishing PD from other parkinsonisms by indicating the presence of endogenous alpha-synuclein sufficient for aggregation upon addition of alpha-synuclein seeds. Currently used in research settings, a blood-based version could extend its clinical application, potentially aiding early-stage or prodromal PD diagnosis.
keyPoints: Alpha-synuclein seeding amplification assay (SAA) provides high sensitivity and specificity.
SAA distinguishes PD from other parkinsonisms.
Potential for a blood-based SAA to aid in clinical diagnosis.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Testing in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Genetic testing is not routinely used for diagnosing PD due to the rarity of monogenic forms, but it can be helpful in specific contexts. It should be considered when there is a strong family history, early onset (<40 years), or particular ethnic backgrounds. GBAI variants are common in PD patients, especially among Ashkenazi Jews.
keyPoints: Monogenic forms of PD account for 5% of cases, increasing to 15% with GBAI variants included.
GBAI variants present in ~10% of PD patients and in 25% of Ashkenazi PD patients.
~20–30% of people with GBAI1 variants will develop PD; risk correlates with variant severity.
LRRK2 gene mutations account for ~3% of sporadic cases, more common (~25%) in Ashkenazi Jews and North African Berber Arabs.
40–50% of LRRK2 carriers never develop clinical features of PD.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Distinguishing Atypical Parkinsonism from PD
items.content: Atypical parkinsonism, including MSA, PSP, and CBS, presents with clinical features distinct from PD. These conditions often involve more widespread pathology and may show early speech/gait involvement, absence of rest tremor, lack of motor asymmetry, poor response to levodopa, and a more aggressive course.
keyPoints: Atypical parkinsonism involves degeneration beyond the SNc, affecting striatum, globus pallidus, cerebellum, and brainstem.
Clinical features differ from PD: early speech/gait involvement, no rest tremor, lack of motor asymmetry, poor levodopa response.
Neuroimaging not helpful; metabolic imaging may show decreased GPi activity with increased thalamic activity (reverse of PD).
MSA shows cerebellar and autonomic features, with MRI showing pathologic iron accumulation or the pontine 'hot cross bun' sign.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Features and Diagnosis of MSA
items.content: MSA is suspected with atypical parkinsonian features plus cerebellar signs or autonomic dysfunction. It progresses more aggressively than PD, characterized by degeneration in multiple brain regions and glial cytoplasmic inclusions.
keyPoints: MSA presents with parkinsonian, cerebellar, or autonomic features; suspected with atypical parkinsonism plus cerebellar signs/autonomic dysfunction.
Poor response to levodopa and orthostatic hypotension are key indicators.
MRI may show pathologic iron accumulation in the striatum or pontine 'hot cross bun' sign.
No established gene mutations or specific treatments for MSA.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 2)

items.title: Distinguishing PSP from Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP) can be differentiated from Parkinson's Disease (PD) by specific clinical features and imaging findings. The 'Parkinson' form of PSP may resemble PD early on but includes slow ocular saccades, eyelid apraxia, restricted vertical eye movements with impaired downward gaze, and hyperextension of the neck. The 'Richardson' form shows little response to levodopa. MRI can reveal a characteristic midbrain atrophy known as the 'hummingbird sign'.
keyPoints: Slow ocular saccades, eyelid apraxia, restricted vertical eye movements with impaired downward gaze.
'Parkinson' form of PSP may respond to levodopa initially, unlike the 'Richardson' form.
MRI shows midbrain atrophy ('hummingbird sign').
PSP involves degeneration in SNc, striatum, STN, midline thalamic nuclei, and pallidum with tau protein deposits.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 3)

items.title: Identifying Corticobasal Syndrome
items.content: Corticobasal Syndrome (CBS) presents with asymmetric dystonic contractions, clumsiness of one hand, and cortical sensory disturbances such as apraxia or agnosia. MRI may show asymmetric cortical atrophy. Pathologically, CBS features achromatic neuronal degeneration with tau deposits. It can overlap clinically and pathologically with PSP.
keyPoints: Asymmetric dystonic contractions, clumsiness of one hand, cortical sensory disturbances (apraxia, agnosia).
MRI may show asymmetric cortical atrophy.
Pathologic findings: achromatic neuronal degeneration with tau deposits.
Overlap with PSP can make differentiation challenging without pathology.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 3)

items.title: Recognizing Secondary Parkinsonisms
items.content: Secondary parkinsonism arises from various etiologies like drugs, stroke, or toxins affecting the basal ganglia. Neuroleptics are common culprits, often used in psychiatry and for GI issues, leading to dopamine-blocking effects. Toxins such as carbon monoxide or manganese damage the globus pallidus, resembling atypical parkinsonism with poor levodopa response.
keyPoints: Caused by drugs (e.g., neuroleptics), stroke, tumor, infection, toxins.
Neuroleptics are a common cause; also include metoclopramide and others like tetrabenzine.
Toxins affecting globus pallidus resemble atypical parkinsonism with poor levodopa response.
Secondary parkinsonism features depend on the specific basal ganglia region affected.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 3)

items.title: Genetic Factors in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Most cases of Parkinson's disease (PD) are sporadic with unknown causes, but gene mutations can be found even in these cases. Genetic factors are more significant in younger-onset PD (<50 years), while environmental factors may play a role in older-onset PD (≥50 years). Recent sequencing technologies have identified monogenic forms of late-onset neurodegenerative diseases, suggesting similar discoveries for PD.
keyPoints: Most PD cases occur sporadically with unknown cause.
Gene mutations are found even in sporadic cases.
Genetic factors more important in younger-onset (<50 years) PD.
Environmental factors may play a role in older-onset (≥50 years) PD.
15% of PD cases are familial.
Monogenic forms can manifest late, into the eighth or ninth decade.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Environmental Hypothesis in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: The environmental hypothesis for PD gained support with MPTP causing a PD-like syndrome, though no direct link to sporadic PD exists. Epidemiologic studies have inconsistently reported increased PD risk with exposure to pesticides, solvents, rural living, farming, and well water. Protective factors include caffeine, cigarette smoking, NSAIDs, and calcium channel blockers.
keyPoints: MPTP caused a PD-like syndrome but not linked to sporadic PD.
Inconsistent reports of increased PD risk with pesticide/solvent exposure.
Possible protective factors: caffeine, smoking, NSAIDs, calcium channel blockers.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Double-Hit Hypothesis in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: The double-hit hypothesis suggests PD results from an interaction between genetic risk factors and environmental toxins. This model proposes that both a genetic predisposition and an environmental trigger are necessary for PD to develop, though direct evidence is lacking.
keyPoints: Double-hit hypothesis: genetic susceptibility + environmental toxin.
No direct evidence supporting the double-hit hypothesis.
Genetic/environmental risk factors alone insufficient to cause PD.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Role of SNCA Gene in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Mutations and gene duplications/triplications in the SNCA gene are linked to PD. These genetic alterations lead to earlier onset, faster progression, and more prominent nonmotor features compared to nongenetic PD.
keyPoints: SNCA mutations cause earlier disease onset than nongenetic PD
Faster progression of motor signs in patients with SNCA mutations
Early occurrence of motor fluctuations and cognitive impairment
Duplication or triplication of wild-type SNCA gene causes PD, with triplication being more severe
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: Prion-Like Mechanism of Alpha-Synuclein in PD
items.content: Alpha-synuclein may act as a prion, misfolding and spreading to cause neurodegeneration. This hypothesis is supported by studies showing Lewy pathology development after injection of alpha-synuclein fibrils.
keyPoints: Alpha-synuclein constitutes the major component of Lewy bodies
Misfolded alpha-synuclein can form toxic oligomers, aggregates, and amyloid plaques
Injection of purified alpha-synuclein fibrils induces Lewy pathology in rodents
Inoculation with human Lewy body homogenates causes dopamine cell degeneration in mice and primates
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: Potential Neuroprotective Therapies Targeting Alpha-Synuclein
items.content: Therapeutic strategies for PD may focus on inhibiting alpha-synuclein accumulation, enhancing its removal, or preventing its spread. Despite ongoing research, immune-based approaches have not yet been successful.
keyPoints: Neuroprotective therapies might inhibit accumulation or enhance removal of toxic alpha-synuclein
Strategies include knocking down SNCA levels and blocking misfolding propagation
Postmortem studies suggest inflammation may promote alpha-synuclein misfolding
Immune-based approaches to clear alpha-synuclein have not been successful in PD
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.title: GBA1 Variants as a Risk Factor for Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Pathogenic variants of the GBA1 gene are significant risk factors for both developing Parkinson's disease (PD) and its severity. These variants impair lysosomal function by reducing glucocerebrosidase activity, leading to $ \alpha $-synuclein accumulation. This creates a vicious cycle that exacerbates PD pathology.
keyPoints: GBA1 gene variants are the most important risk factor for PD development and severity.
Reduced GCase activity due to GBA1 variants impairs lysosomal function, causing $ \alpha $-synuclein accumulation.
This molecular pathway is relevant not only in patients with GBA1 variants but also those with sporadic PD or other synucleinopathies.
Patients with certain GBA1 variants (e.g., L444P) may experience faster disease progression and increased cognitive impairment.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.title: LRRK2 Mutations and Their Role in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Multiple pathogenic mutations in LRRK2, particularly p.G2019S, are linked to PD. These variants account for a significant portion of familial cases and some sporadic ones. The penetrance is incomplete, with patients often experiencing a more benign course than those with idiopathic PD.
keyPoints: LRRK2 mutations, especially p.G2019S, are common in certain populations like Ashkenazi Jews and North African Arabs.
Pathogenic variants in LRRK2 account for 2-41% of familial PD cases.
Penetrance is incomplete (30-74%), with a more benign course compared to idiopathic PD.
Enhanced kinase activity from these mutations may impair lysosomal function, contributing to cell death.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.title: Potential Therapeutic Targets in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Therapies targeting GCase activity and LRRK2 kinase inhibition are being explored for PD. Enhancing GCase activity may mitigate $ \alpha $-synuclein accumulation, while selective LRRK2 inhibitors show promise with good preclinical safety.
keyPoints: Drug and gene-based therapies enhancing GCase activity are under clinical testing as neuroprotective treatments.
LRRK2 kinase inhibitors can block toxicity associated with pathogenic variants.
Nonselective kinase inhibitors pose risks to lungs and kidneys, but selective LRRK2 inhibitors have good preclinical safety profiles.
c-Abl inhibitors targeting $ \alpha $-synuclein phosphorylation are of particular interest.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.title: Role of PRKN Mutations in Early-Onset Parkinson's Disease
items.content: PRKN mutations are a significant cause of autosomal recessive early-onset PD, particularly in juvenile cases. These mutations lead to a slowly progressive form of the disease that responds well to antiparkinsonian treatment but may be complicated by dystonia and rarely dementia.
keyPoints: PRKN mutations account for up to 77% of juvenile PD patients with onset <20 years.
They cause 10–20% of early-onset PD cases in general.
Disease progression is slow, responds well to treatment, often complicated by dystonia, rarely dementia.
Pathological neurodegeneration is mainly restricted to the SNc and LC; Lewy bodies present in ~20%.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.title: Mitochondrial Dysfunction as a Therapeutic Target in PD
items.content: Mutations in Parkin and PINK1 lead to mitochondrial dysfunction, which is linked to the pathogenesis of PD. Improving mitochondrial function could be a therapeutic target, given that complex I defects are observed in SNc neurons of PD patients.
keyPoints: Parkin and PINK1 mutations cause mitochondrial dysfunction.
Mitochondrial dysfunction can be corrected with parkin overexpression in animal models.
Postmortem studies show defect in complex I of the respiratory chain in SNc neurons.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.title: Inflammation and Immune System Involvement in PD Pathogenesis
items.content: Inflammation and altered immunity are implicated as key factors in PD pathogenesis. Genetic associations with HLA gene DRB1 variants, presence of autoreactive T cells against α-synuclein, and potential therapeutic effects of drugs like sargramostim highlight the role of immune mechanisms.
keyPoints: Association of PD with class II HLA gene DRB1 (variants linked to risk or protection).
Inflammation occurs years before α-synuclein aggregates in transplanted patients, suggesting a triggering role.
Autoreactive T cells recognizing α-synuclein peptides present in PD patients.
Sargramostim upregulates T-regulatory cells with positive results in animal models.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.title: Role of Dopaminergic Projections in Parkinson's Disease Pathophysiology
items.content: Dopamine projections from the substantia nigra pars compacta (SNc) modulate neuronal firing within the basal ganglia, facilitating movement selection and suppressing unwanted movements. In Parkinson's Disease (PD), dopamine denervation leads to increased activity in the subthalamic nucleus (STN) and globus pallidus interna (GPI), resulting in excessive inhibition of thalamic activity and reduced cortical motor system activation.
keyPoints: Dopaminergic projections from SNc modulate neuronal firing within basal ganglia.
In PD, dopamine denervation leads to increased STN and GPI activity.
This results in excessive thalamic inhibition and reduced cortical motor activation.
Bedside pearl: Understanding these mechanisms helps explain the motor deficits seen in PD.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF PD

items.title: Surgical Interventions for Parkinson's Disease Based on Basal Ganglia Pathophysiology
items.content: The pathophysiological model of basal ganglia organization suggests that lesions or high-frequency stimulation of the STN or GPI can reduce their inhibitory effect on thalamocortical pathways, potentially improving PD symptoms. This approach is based on the understanding that increased activity in these regions leads to parkinsonian features.
keyPoints: Lesions or high-frequency stimulation of STN/GPI may improve PD symptoms.
This intervention targets the inhibitory effect on thalamocortical pathways.
The model predicts symptom improvement by altering basal ganglia output.
Bedside pearl: Surgical options are informed by this pathophysiological understanding.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY OF PD

items.title: Impact of SARS-CoV-2 on Parkinson's Disease Features and Outcomes
items.content: SARS-CoV-2 infection can exacerbate Parkinson's disease (PD) symptoms, such as worsening PD features and increasing 'off' time. Additionally, patients with PD face higher risks of complications and mortality from COVID-19.
keyPoints: Worsening of PD features and increased off time due to SARS-CoV-2 infection
Higher risk of complications and death in PD patients with COVID-19
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · COVID-19 AND PD

items.title: SARS-CoV-2 Entry into the Brain and New-Onset Parkinson's Disease
items.content: The SARS-CoV-2 virus can enter the brain, causing inflammation and microglial activation. This has been linked to new-onset cases of Parkinson's disease following infection.
keyPoints: SARS-CoV-2 entry into the brain leading to inflammation and microglial activation
Reported new-onset PD cases post-SARS-CoV-2 infection
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · COVID-19 AND PD

items.title: Why Levodopa is the Gold Standard in PD Treatment
items.content: Levodopa, introduced in the late 1960s, remains the most effective symptomatic treatment for Parkinson's Disease (PD). It benefits both classic motor features and some nondopaminergic symptoms like anxiety and depression. Almost all PD patients experience improvement with levodopa, making it the benchmark against which new therapies are compared.
keyPoints: Levodopa is the mainstay of therapy since its introduction in the late 1960s.
Benefits classic motor features and some nondopaminergic symptoms such as anxiety and depression.
Almost all PD patients experience improvement with levodopa; failure to respond should prompt reevaluation of diagnosis.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · LEVODOPA (part 1)

items.title: How Levodopa is Administered to Minimize Side Effects
items.content: Levodopa is combined with a peripheral decarboxylase inhibitor to prevent its metabolism to dopamine outside the brain, which can cause nausea and orthostatic hypotension. In the U.S., it's combined with carbidopa (Sinemet), while in other countries, benserazide (Madopar) is used.
keyPoints: Levodopa is combined with a peripheral decarboxylase inhibitor to prevent peripheral metabolism and side effects like nausea and orthostatic hypotension.
In the U.S., levodopa is combined with carbidopa (Sinemet); in other countries, it's often combined with benserazide (Madopar).
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · LEVODOPA (part 1)

items.title: Limitations and Motor Complications of Chronic Levodopa Use
items.content: Chronic levodopa use is associated with motor complications such as fluctuations in response ('on' and 'off' episodes) and dyskinesias. Initially, benefits are long-lasting, but over time, the duration of benefit shortens (wearing-off effect). Advanced cases may experience unpredictable responses to doses.
keyPoints: Chronic levodopa use leads to motor complications like fluctuations ('on' and 'off' episodes) and dyskinesias.
Initial benefits are long-lasting but shorten over time, known as the wearing-off effect.
Advanced cases may have unpredictable responses to doses (full-on, partial-on, delayed-on, no-on).
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · LEVODOPA (part 1)

items.title: Minimizing Motor Complications in Levodopa Therapy
items.content: To minimize the risk of motor complications, such as dyskinesias, it is recommended to use the lowest effective dose of levodopa and employ polypharmacy strategies.
keyPoints: Motor complications are more likely with higher doses of levodopa.
Younger individuals, females, and those with severe disease are at increased risk.
Long-term studies suggest minimizing motor complications by using the lowest effective dose and avoiding increasing levodopa dosage through polypharmacy.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · LEVODOPA (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Levodopa-Induced Dyskinesias
items.content: Levodopa-induced dyskinesias are thought to result from alterations in neuronal firing patterns rather than just frequency, leading to misinformation transmission to motor systems.
keyPoints: Dyskinesia involves changes in GPI/SNR neuronal firing patterns (pauses, bursts, synchrony).
Classic basal ganglia model suggests excessive inhibition of pallidal output leads to dyskinesias.
Surgical or ultrasound lesions/high-frequency stimulation at GPI/STN can ameliorate dyskinesia by blocking abnormal activity.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · LEVODOPA (part 2)

items.title: Continuous Levodopa Delivery to Reduce Motor Complications
items.content: Continuous delivery of levodopa, such as intraintestinal infusion or subcutaneous infusion, can significantly improve 'off' time and reduce dyskinesias compared to intermittent oral doses.
keyPoints: Intermittent oral levodopa causes fluctuating plasma levels leading to motor complications.
Continuous delivery methods (intraintestinal or subcutaneous) show improvement in 'on' time without troublesome dyskinesia.
Benefits of continuous delivery are superior to placebo-controlled studies with other dopaminergic agents.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · LEVODOPA (part 2)

items.title: Levodopa's Role in Symptomatic Relief vs. Disease Progression
items.content: Levodopa is primarily used for its symptomatic benefits in Parkinson's disease, balancing these against the risk of motor complications and other side effects. There is no established evidence that levodopa has a neuroprotective effect or accelerates neuronal degeneration.
keyPoints: Levodopa provides symptomatic relief but does not alter disease progression.
Concerns about potential neurotoxicity from oxidative metabolism are unproven.
Recent studies show no significant impact on clinical progression rates.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · LEVODOPA (part 3) | DOPAMINE AGONISTS

items.title: Dopamine Agonists as Adjunct Therapy in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Dopamine agonists are used to enhance motor function and reduce 'off' time, particularly in younger patients at risk of developing motor complications. They are less likely than levodopa to induce dyskinesia but have serious side effects like impulse control disorders.
keyPoints: Dopamine agonists act directly on dopamine receptors without needing metabolic conversion.
They are less effective than levodopa but reduce the risk of dyskinesia.
Common adverse effects include sleep episodes and impulse control disorders.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · LEVODOPA (part 3) | DOPAMINE AGONISTS

items.title: Apomorphine's Use in Managing Severe 'Off' Episodes
items.content: Apomorphine, a dopamine agonist with efficacy comparable to levodopa, is used for severe 'off' episodes due to its short half-life. It can be administered subcutaneously or via continuous infusion and has a sublingual formulation.
keyPoints: Apomorphine requires parenteral administration due to rapid oral metabolism.
Effective for acute management of 'off' periods with a 45-minute duration of action.
Sublingual bilayer formulation offers an alternative to injections.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · LEVODOPA (part 3) | DOPAMINE AGONISTS

items.title: Role and Safety of MAO-B Inhibitors in Parkinson's Disease Management
items.content: MAO-B inhibitors, such as selegiline and rasagiline, are used to increase synaptic dopamine by inhibiting its oxidative metabolism. They provide benefits for early-stage Parkinson's disease (PD) when used alone and reduce 'off' time in patients with motor fluctuations when combined with levodopa. Safinamide, a reversible MAO-B inhibitor, also offers potential antidyskinetic effects due to additional mechanisms like blocking sodium channels and inhibiting glutamate release.
keyPoints: Selegiline and rasagiline are selective for MAO-B, reducing 'off' time in levodopa-treated patients.
Safinamide has dual action: reversible MAO-B inhibition and antidyskinetic effects by blocking sodium channels and inhibiting glutamate release.
No cheese effect with currently used doses due to lack of MAO-A inhibition.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · MAO-B INHIBITORS | COMT INHIBITORS

items.title: Clinical Use and Monitoring of COMT Inhibitors in PD Treatment
items.content: COMT inhibitors, including tolcapone, entacapone, and opicapone, enhance levodopa's effectiveness by increasing its brain availability. They reduce 'off' time and improve motor scores but can cause side effects like nausea and dyskinesia, typically managed by adjusting the levodopa dose. Tolcapone requires liver function monitoring due to rare cases of hepatic toxicity.
keyPoints: COMT inhibitors prolong levodopa's half-life and enhance its brain availability.
Side effects include nausea, vomiting, increased dyskinesia, and severe diarrhea with tolcapone in 5-10% of patients.
Liver function monitoring is necessary for tolcapone due to rare hepatic toxicity.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · MAO-B INHIBITORS | COMT INHIBITORS

items.title: Evaluating the Initiation Strategy of Levodopa with COMT Inhibitors in PD
items.content: Initiating levodopa with a COMT inhibitor may provide continuous delivery and reduce motor complications, as shown in preclinical models. However, no benefit was observed in early PD patients in the STRIDE-PD study, possibly due to insufficient dosing frequency for continuous availability.
keyPoints: Continuous levodopa delivery via COMT inhibitors reduces 'off' time and dyskinesia in advanced PD.
STRIDE-PD study found no benefit of initiating levodopa with a COMT inhibitor in early PD patients.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · MAO-B INHIBITORS | COMT INHIBITORS

items.title: Role of A2A Receptor Antagonists in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Adenosine A2A receptor antagonists inhibit A2A receptors, which form heterodimers with D2 dopamine receptors on medium spiny striatal neurons. This blockade decreases excessive activation of the indirect pathway in PD, theoretically restoring balance in the basal ganglia-thalamocortical circuit and providing a dopaminergic effect without increasing levodopa doses.
keyPoints: A2A receptor antagonists decrease excessive activation of the indirect pathway in PD.
Istradefylline is the only A2A antagonist currently approved for use.
Clinical trials showed improvement in 'off' time comparable to other agents but not in dyskinesia.
Caffeine, a potent A2A antagonist, is associated with reduced frequency of PD.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES

items.title: Amantadine's Use and Effects in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Amantadine, originally an antiviral agent, has antiparkinsonian effects due to NMDA receptor antagonism. It is used primarily as an antidyskinesia agent in advanced PD, reducing dyskinesia without worsening parkinsonian features and sometimes improving motor benefits.
keyPoints: Amantadine is the only oral agent demonstrated to reduce dyskinesia without worsening parkinsonian features.
Cognitive impairment is a major concern with high doses.
Side effects include livedo reticularis and weight gain.
Should be discontinued gradually due to withdrawal-like symptoms.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · OTHER MEDICAL THERAPIES

items.subtopic: CONTINUOUS DOPAMINERGIC DELIVERY | ON-DEMAND THERAPIES FOR OFF PERIODS | NEUROPROTECTION
items.title: Continuous Dopaminergic Delivery Improves Motor Complications in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Intermittent oral doses of short-acting levodopa can lead to nonphysiologic restoration of brain dopamine, causing motor complications. Continuous delivery methods like intraintestinal and subcutaneous infusion provide more stable plasma levels, reducing 'off' time and increasing 'on' time without troublesome dyskinesia.
keyPoints: Continuous delivery methods (intraintestinal, subcutaneous) stabilize plasma levodopa levels.
Reduces 'off' time and increases 'on' time without troublesome dyskinesia.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · CONTINUOUS DOPAMINERGIC DELIVERY | ON-DEMAND THERAPIES FOR OFF PERIODS | NEUROPROTECTION

items.subtopic: CONTINUOUS DOPAMINERGIC DELIVERY | ON-DEMAND THERAPIES FOR OFF PERIODS | NEUROPROTECTION
items.title: On-Demand Therapies for Managing 'Off' Periods in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Despite continuous delivery, many patients experience disabling 'off' periods. Approved on-demand therapies include inhaled levodopa, subcutaneous apomorphine, and sublingual apomorphine. These treatments are fast-acting and provide a predictable return to the 'on' state.
keyPoints: Approved on-demand therapies: inhaled levodopa, subcutaneous apomorphine, sublingual apomorphine.
Fast-acting with predictable return to 'on' state.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · CONTINUOUS DOPAMINERGIC DELIVERY | ON-DEMAND THERAPIES FOR OFF PERIODS | NEUROPROTECTION

items.subtopic: CONTINUOUS DOPAMINERGIC DELIVERY | ON-DEMAND THERAPIES FOR OFF PERIODS | NEUROPROTECTION
items.title: Neuroprotection in Parkinson's Disease: Current Insights and Challenges
items.content: Neuroprotective therapies aim to slow or stop disease progression. Some trials suggest positive effects from agents like selegiline and rasagiline, but definitive proof of neuroprotection is lacking. The ADAGIO study showed early treatment with rasagiline 1 mg/d had benefits not seen with delayed treatment, suggesting a potential disease-modifying effect.
keyPoints: Neuroprotective therapies aim to slow or stop PD progression.
ADAGIO study: Early rasagiline 1 mg/d treatment showed potential disease-modifying effects.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · CONTINUOUS DOPAMINERGIC DELIVERY | ON-DEMAND THERAPIES FOR OFF PERIODS | NEUROPROTECTION

items.title: Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) as a Preferred Surgical Treatment in PD
items.content: DBS is favored over lesioning techniques for treating Parkinson's Disease due to its adjustable nature and ability to provide bilateral treatment without permanent brain damage.
keyPoints: Lesions in the VIM nucleus reduce contralateral tremor but not other disabling features of PD.
Pallidotomy improves rigidity, bradykinesia, and tremor; also reduces dyskinesia but is limited by bilateral side effects such as dysphagia, dysarthria, and impaired cognition.
DBS targets the STN or GPi to provide antiparkinsonian benefits, particularly for severe tremor or levodopa-induced motor complications.
DBS can be adjusted in terms of electrode configuration, voltage, frequency, and pulse duration; it is reversible if no benefit or intolerable side effects occur.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · SURGICAL TREATMENT

items.title: Indications and Benefits of DBS in PD Management
items.content: DBS is indicated for patients with severe tremor or levodopa-induced motor complications that are not manageable through medication adjustments.
keyPoints: DBS provides benefits similar to those of lesions but without the permanence, allowing for bilateral treatment and reversibility.
It reduces 'off' time and dyskinesias but does not surpass levodopa in antiparkinsonian benefits.
GPi stimulation may be associated with a reduced frequency of depression compared to STN stimulation.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · SURGICAL TREATMENT

items.title: Considerations and Future Directions for DBS in PD
items.content: While DBS is beneficial, its use must be carefully considered against emerging therapies and the evolving landscape of PD treatment.
keyPoints: DBS does not prevent nondopaminergic features which continue to evolve as PD progresses.
Future therapies might offer continuous levodopa availability with fewer motor complications, potentially reducing DBS utility.
New targets for DBS are being explored for gait dysfunction, depression, cognitive impairment, and the development of 'smart' closed-loop devices.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · SURGICAL TREATMENT

items.title: Challenges and Concerns with Cell-Based Therapies for Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Cell-based therapies, such as fetal nigral cell transplantation and stem cell use, aim to replace degenerating SNc dopamine neurons. However, these approaches have shown limited success in clinical trials and are associated with significant challenges.
keyPoints: Fetal nigral cell transplantation failed to show significant benefit over sham operations in double-blind studies.
Graft-induced dyskinesia is a persistent side effect even after adjusting or discontinuing levodopa.
Transplanted cells may develop PD pathology and become dysfunctional, suggesting prion-like transfer of α-synuclein.
Stem cell therapies have not been properly tested in double-blind studies and carry risks like malignant transformation.
Replacing dopamine cells does not address nondopaminergic features such as falling and dementia.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · OTHER EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES FOR PD

items.title: Limitations of Trophic Factors in Parkinson's Disease Treatment
items.content: Trophic factors like GDNF and neurturin have shown potential in laboratory studies but failed to demonstrate significant clinical benefits in double-blind trials due to delivery challenges.
keyPoints: Double-blind trials of intraventricular and intraputaminal infusions of GDNF did not benefit PD patients, possibly due to inadequate delivery.
AAV2-mediated gene therapy for neurturin showed promise in open-label trials but failed in double-blind trials.
Long-term studies indicate transgene survival up to 10 years post-treatment, though coverage and dosage may need improvement.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · OTHER EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES FOR PD

items.title: Potential and Risks of Gene Therapy in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Gene therapy offers long-term expression of therapeutic proteins but faces challenges such as delivery efficacy and addressing nondopaminergic symptoms.
keyPoints: AAV2 is commonly used for gene therapy due to its safety profile, including lack of inflammatory response and insertional mutagenesis.
Gene therapy approaches include delivering enzymes to convert levodopa to dopamine and enhancing GCase to clear misfolded α-synuclein.
No significant adverse events have been reported in CNS-directed gene therapy studies, but risks like mutagenesis remain.
Current gene therapies targeting the dopamine system may not address nondopaminergic features of PD.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · OTHER EXPERIMENTAL THERAPIES FOR PD

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 1)
items.title: Managing Depression and Anxiety in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Depression is common, affecting approximately 50% of PD patients, often underdiagnosed and undertreated. Antidepressants should be used for major depression, while dopaminergic agents like pramipexole can help with both depression and motor symptoms. Anxiety, if not controlled by antiparkinsonian therapies, may require short-acting benzodiazepines.
keyPoints: Approximately 50% of PD patients suffer from depression during the course of the disease.
Antidepressants should be used for major depression in PD patients.
Dopaminergic agents such as pramipexole may help with both depression and motor symptoms.
Anxiety can be treated with short-acting benzodiazepines if not controlled by antiparkinsonian therapies.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 1)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 1)
items.title: Approach to Psychosis in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Psychosis, often a precursor to dementia, is usually visual and nonthreatening. Management involves withdrawing less effective dopaminergic agents like anticholinergics, amantadine, and dopamine agonists before reducing levodopa doses. Low-dose atypical neuroleptics can help manage psychosis, with clozapine being effective but requiring regular monitoring due to agranulocytosis risk. Quetiapine avoids this issue but lacks placebo-controlled trial evidence. Pimavanserin is an alternative but has mild efficacy and risks of QT prolongation and death in elderly patients.
keyPoints: Psychosis in PD often involves visual, formed, nonthreatening hallucinations.
Initial management includes withdrawing anticholinergics, amantadine, and dopamine agonists before reducing levodopa doses.
Low-dose atypical neuroleptics can manage psychosis; clozapine is effective but requires monitoring for agranulocytosis.
Quetiapine avoids agranulocytosis risk but lacks placebo-controlled trial evidence.
Pimavanserin has mild efficacy and risks of QT prolongation and death in elderly patients.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 1)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 1)
items.title: Cognitive Impairment and Dementia in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Dementia affects up to 80% of PD patients, primarily impacting executive functions and attention. Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) occurs when dementia precedes or develops within a year after motor symptoms, while PD dementia follows more than a year later. GBA1 gene variants increase risk for both PD and DLB. Mild cognitive impairment often precedes dementia in PD. Treatment involves reducing drugs that worsen cognition, starting with anticholinergics, amantadine, dopamine agonists, COMT inhibitors, and MAO-B inhibitors. Memantine and rivastigmine can improve attention but not cognition or quality of life significantly.
keyPoints: Dementia affects up to 80% of PD patients, primarily impacting executive functions and attention.
DLB occurs when dementia precedes or develops within a year after motor symptoms; PD dementia follows more than a year later.
GBA1 gene variants are significant risk factors for both PD and DLB.
Mild cognitive impairment often precedes dementia in PD patients.
Drug reduction sequence: anticholinergics, amantadine, dopamine agonists, COMT inhibitors, MAO-B inhibitors.
Memantine and rivastigmine improve attention but not cognition or quality of life significantly.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 1)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 2)
items.title: Management of Orthostatic Hypotension in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Orthostatic hypotension is a common autonomic disturbance in Parkinson's disease that can contribute to falls. Initial management includes dietary salt addition and head-of-bed elevation. Pharmacological treatments such as fludrocortisone, midodrine, or 3-0-methylDOPS (Droxidopa) are recommended for most cases. In severe or refractory cases, vasopressin and erythropoietin may be used. Consider MSA if orthostatic hypotension is prominent early in parkinsonian cases.
keyPoints: Orthostatic hypotension can contribute to falls.
Initial treatment: add salt to diet, elevate head of bed.
Pharmacological options include fludrocortisone, midodrine, 3-0-methylDOPS (mild but transient benefits).
Severe cases may require vasopressin or erythropoietin.
Consider MSA if orthostatic hypotension is prominent early.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 2)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 2)
items.title: Approach to Sleep Disturbances in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Sleep disturbances, including fragmented sleep and excessive daytime sleepiness, are common in Parkinson's disease. These issues can be exacerbated by dopamine agonists and may relate to circadian rhythm alterations due to degeneration of melanopsin-containing neurons and suprachiasmatic nucleus cells. Light therapy with specific wavelengths may improve both motor and nonmotor features by restoring circadian rhythms. REM behavior disorder (RBD) often precedes classic PD symptoms, and low doses of clonazepam are effective for management. Consultation with a sleep specialist and polysomnography may be necessary.
keyPoints: Sleep disturbances include fragmented sleep and excessive daytime sleepiness.
Dopamine agonists can exacerbate these problems.
Circadian rhythm alterations due to degeneration in melanopsin-containing neurons and suprachiasmatic nucleus cells.
Light therapy may improve motor and nonmotor features.
RBD often precedes PD symptoms; low doses of clonazepam (0.5–1 mg) are effective.
Consultation with a sleep specialist and polysomnography may be necessary.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · MANAGEMENT OF NONMOTOR AND NONDOPAMINERGIC FEATURES OF PD (part 2)

items.title: Gait Dysfunction Management in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: In Parkinson's disease (PD), gait dysfunction is a significant cause of disability, often leading to falls. While dopaminergic therapies may help during 'off' periods, there are no specific treatments for gait dysfunction itself. Nonpharmacologic interventions such as canes and walkers are recommended to enhance stability and reduce fall risk.
keyPoints: Gait dysfunction is a major cause of disability in PD.
Dopaminergic therapies may help during 'off' periods but not specifically for gait dysfunction.
Canes and walkers are necessary to increase stability and reduce the risk of falling.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · NONPHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY

items.title: Addressing Freezing Phenomenon in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Freezing, where patients temporarily become immobile as if their feet are stuck to the ground, is a common issue in PD and contributes significantly to falls. While freezing during 'off' periods may respond to dopaminergic therapies, there are no specific treatments for freezing that occurs during 'on' periods. Sensory cues such as marching or stepping over an imaginary line can be effective strategies.
keyPoints: Freezing is a major cause of falling in PD patients.
Occurs during both 'on' and 'off' periods, with no specific treatments for 'on' period freezing.
Sensory cues like marching or stepping over an imaginary line may help alleviate freezing.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · NONPHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY

items.title: Timing of Disease-Modifying Therapy in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Initiating disease-modifying therapy at the time of diagnosis or during the premotor stage is crucial for maximizing potential benefits. Striatal dopamine terminal degeneration may be complete within 4 years of diagnosis, limiting the effectiveness of therapies started later.
keyPoints: Striatal dopamine terminal degeneration may be complete within 4 years of diagnosis.
Premotor features such as constipation, RBD, and anosmia could allow earlier diagnosis and treatment initiation.
Rasagiline 1 mg/d met all three prespecified primary endpoints consistent with disease-modifying effects.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · CURRENT MANAGEMENT OF PD (part 1)

items.title: Initiation of Symptomatic Therapy in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: Symptomatic therapy should be initiated at diagnosis to preserve compensatory mechanisms and improve quality of life. Levodopa is the most effective, recommended starting at low doses ($ ≤ $400 mg/d) due to dose-related motor complications.
keyPoints: Start symptomatic therapy at diagnosis for potential functional benefits.
Levodopa remains the most effective symptomatic therapy; start with low doses ($ ≤ $400 mg/d).
Consider starting with MAO-B inhibitors or dopamine agonists to delay levodopa introduction.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · CURRENT MANAGEMENT OF PD (part 1)

items.title: Polypharmacy in Parkinson's Disease Management
items.content: Most patients benefit from polypharmacy, combining levodopa with an MAO-B inhibitor and a dopamine agonist to minimize daily levodopa dose and reduce motor complications. Low doses of each agent are recommended to mitigate side effects.
keyPoints: Polypharmacy can minimize total daily levodopa dose and reduce motor complications.
Use low doses of each medication to reduce the risk of side effects.
Patients should not be denied levodopa if alternative medications do not provide adequate control.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · CURRENT MANAGEMENT OF PD (part 1)

items.title: Management Strategies for Motor Complications in Parkinson's Disease
items.content: When motor complications arise in Parkinson's disease, initial management includes adjusting the frequency and dose of levodopa or combining it with a dopamine agonist, COMT inhibitor, or MAO-B inhibitor. An A2A antagonist like istrade-fylline can also be used for off periods. On-demand therapies such as subcutaneous apomorphine, inhaled levodopa, and sublingual apomorphine are options to treat individual off periods and may delay surgical interventions.
keyPoints: Adjusting levodopa frequency/dose or combining with dopamine agonist, COMT inhibitor, or MAO-B inhibitor can manage motor complications.
A2A antagonist (e.g., istrade-fylline) is used for off periods.
On-demand therapies: subcutaneous apomorphine, inhaled levodopa, and sublingual apomorphine treat individual off periods.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · CURRENT MANAGEMENT OF PD (part 2)

items.title: Surgical and Continuous Therapies for Advanced Parkinson's Disease
items.content: In advanced Parkinson's disease with uncontrolled motor complications, surgical options like DBS or continuous dopaminergic therapies such as Duodopa or subcutaneous infusion of levodopa/apomorphine may be considered. However, these interventions have potential complications and are debated for early-stage patients due to costs and side effects.
keyPoints: DBS and continuous therapies (Duodopa, subcutaneous infusion) are options for advanced PD with uncontrolled motor complications.
DBS is controversial in early PD due to cost and potential side effects.
Continuous intraintestinal levodopa/carbidopa gel requires surgery with serious risks.
Subcutaneous infusion can cause skin issues and requires wearing an infusion pump.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · CURRENT MANAGEMENT OF PD (part 2)

items.subtopic: Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS
items.question: What is considered the hallmark pathological feature of Parkinson's disease?
options: Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta and Lewy pathology.
Loss of serotonin neurons in the raphe nuclei only.
Hyperactivity of norepinephrine neurons in the locus coeruleus.
items.explanation: The hallmark pathological features include degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta and Lewy pathology, which includes α-synuclein inclusions known as Lewy bodies and neurites.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS

items.subtopic: Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cardinal clinical feature of Parkinson's disease?
items.explanation: The cardinal clinical features of Parkinson's disease include bradykinesia, rest tremor, rigidity, and gait dysfunction with postural instability. Hyperreflexia is not a feature of PD.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS

items.subtopic: Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS
items.question: Which age group can present with Parkinson's disease due to pathogenic gene mutations?
options: Individuals in their twenties and even younger.
items.explanation: Parkinson's disease can be seen in individuals in their twenties and even younger, particularly when associated with a pathogenic gene mutation.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS

items.subtopic: Parkinson's Disease | PARKINSON'S DISEASE AND RELATED DISORDERS
items.question: What is the estimated lifetime risk of developing Parkinson's disease for men?
items.explanation: The estimated lifetime risk of developing Parkinson's disease is ~3% for men.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a feature used to increase confidence in diagnosing Parkinson's disease according to the MDS Clinical Diagnostic Criteria?

items.question: Which imaging technique shows reduced and asymmetric uptake in the striatum, particularly in the posterior putamen, in Parkinson's disease?
items.explanation: PET or SPECT imaging shows reduced and asymmetric uptake in the striatum, particularly in the posterior putamen.

items.question: What is the primary reason for the high error rate historically found when diagnosing Parkinson's disease based on clinical criteria alone?
options: Overlap in symptoms with other neurodegenerative diseases
items.explanation: The high error rate was due to inconsistent clinical criteria, particularly the lack of requirement for a good response to levodopa.
items.reference: Parkinson s Disease · DIAGNOSIS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS (part 1)

items.question: Which assay is noted for its ability to distinguish Parkinson's disease from other parkinsonian conditions with high sensitivity and specificity?
items.explanation: The $\alpha$-synuclein seeding amplification assay (SAA) is noted for its high sensitivity and specificity in distinguishing PD.

items.question: Which genetic variant is most commonly associated with Parkinson's Disease?
items.explanation: Genetic variants of GBAI are the most common genetic association with PD.

items.question: In which population is LRRK2 mutations a particularly common cause of Parkinson's Disease?
options: Both Ashkenazi Jews and North African Berber Arabs
items.explanation: LRRK2 mutations are a particularly common cause of PD in Ashkenazi Jews and North African Berber Arabs.

items.question: What percentage of people with GBAI1 variants will develop Parkinson's Disease?
items.explanation: Only ~20–30% of people with GBAI1 variants will develop PD.

items.question: Which clinical feature is typically absent in atypical parkinsonism compared to classical Parkinson's Disease?

items.question: Which clinical feature is characteristic of Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP) but not Parkinson's Disease?

items.question: Which gene mutation has been detected in some familial cases of Progressive Supranuclear Palsy?

items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with secondary parkinsonism due to dopamine-blocking agents?
items.explanation: Dopamine-blocking agents such as neuroleptics are the most common cause of secondary parkinsonism.

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature between Progressive Supranuclear Palsy and Corticobasal Syndrome?
options: PSP has restricted vertical eye movements, CBS has asymmetric dystonic contractions
CBS responds well to levodopa, PSP does not
Both are primarily caused by mutations in the MAPT gene

items.question: Which gene mutations have been identified as causing late-onset Parkinson's disease?
items.explanation: Gene mutations such as LRRK2 and GBA1 have been identified to cause late-onset Parkinson's disease.

items.question: What was the environmental agent linked to a PD syndrome in addicts during the 1980s?
items.explanation: MPTP, a by-product of illicit drug manufacture, caused a Parkinson's disease syndrome in addicts.

items.question: Which genetic variants are associated with an increased risk of developing PD?
items.explanation: Variants in the genes SNCA, LRRK2, MAPT, and GBA1 are associated with an increased risk of PD.

items.question: What is the proposed 'double-hit' hypothesis for Parkinson's disease?
options: Two genetic mutations are required to cause PD.
An interaction between genetic risk factors and environmental exposure may lead to PD.
PD can be caused by caffeine intake alone.
Only environmental factors are responsible for PD.
items.explanation: The 'double-hit' hypothesis suggests that both genetic susceptibility and toxic environmental exposure are required for Parkinson's disease to develop.

items.question: Which genetic alteration of the SNCA gene is associated with a more severe form of Parkinson's disease?

items.question: What is the major component of Lewy bodies implicated in both genetic and sporadic forms of Parkinson's disease?

items.question: What is a proposed mechanism by which $ \alpha $-synuclein pathology could spread to cause Parkinson's disease?
options: Gut-brain axis via the vagus nerve

items.question: Which gene is most commonly associated with both the development and severity of Parkinson's disease?
items.explanation: Pathogenic variants of the GBA1 gene represent the most important risk factor for both the development and severity of PD.

items.question: Which LRRK2 mutation is most common and possibly due to a founder effect in certain populations?

items.question: What is the potential mechanism of cell death associated with LRRK2 pathogenic variants?
items.explanation: The mechanism responsible for cell death with LRRK2 pathogenic variants is likely due to enhanced kinase activity.

items.question: Which gene mutation is most commonly associated with autosomal recessive early-onset Parkinson's disease?

items.question: What is a characteristic pathological feature in patients with PRKN mutations?
options: Lewy bodies present in ~80% of brains
Neurodegeneration restricted to SNc and LC
Presence of Lewy bodies in all cases

items.question: What is a potential role of parkin related to Lewy bodies?
options: Parkin promotes the formation of Lewy bodies
Parkin prevents the formation of Lewy bodies
Ubiquitination by parkin may be required for incorporation into Lewy bodies
Parkin has no relation to Lewy bodies

items.question: What cellular dysfunction is observed postmortem in PD patients' SNc neurons?

items.question: Which region of the basal ganglia serves as the major input region?

items.question: What effect does increased neuronal activity in the output regions (GPI/SNr) of the basal ganglia have on movement?

items.question: What role do dopaminergic projections from SNc neurons play in the basal ganglia network?
options: Facilitate selection of desired movements and suppression of unwanted ones
Increase neuronal firing in STN and GPI
